coredata.dc:title: Computational investigation on the geometry and electronic structures and absorption spectra of metal-porphyrin-oligo- phenyleneethynylenes-[60] fullerene triads
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Tale Moghim, Masoud
Jamehbozorgi, Saeed
Rezvani, Mahyar
Ramezani, Majid
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we focus our attention on the influence of 2nd-row transition metals on the structural geometries, electronic structures, and absorption characteristics of porphyrin linked with the C60 fullerene with oligo-p-phenyleneethynylenes (MP-C60-oligo-PPEs) compounds. The DFT/B3PW91-D3 and CAM-B3LYP-D3/6-31G (d) calculations revealed that various metals embedded within the porphyrin moiety give different bridge conformations and different HOMO-LUMO energy levels. We calculate the UV–Vis spectra and absorption parameters using the time-dependent ZINDO/S approach. Our findings indicate that all the compounds have enhanced absorptions in the visible light range, and their molecular orbital energies adopt the condition of sensitizers. However, all of the complexes except down spin states exhibit considerably charge spatial separation. The results suggest that the ZnP-C60-oligo-PPEs triad can meet the necessary conditions of the sensitizer of dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) in comparison with other counterparts and could be an optimum triad compound for potential application in photovoltaic devices.

Simulated absorption spectra for the MP-C60-oligo-PPEs compounds with TD-ZINDO/S.
Comparative simulated absorption spectra for the Porphyrin, C60, and Oligo-PPEs compounds with TD-ZINDO/S and TD-B3PW91 methods.

Representation of energy diagrams for MP-C60-oligo-PPEs compounds, (a) ScP-C60-oligo-PPEs (Up spin), (b) ScP-C60-oligo-PPEs (Down spin), (c) TiP-C60-oligo-PPEs, (d) VP-C60-oligo-PPEs (Up spin), (e) VP-C60-oligo-PPEs (Down spin), (f) CrP-C60-oligo-PPEs, (g) MnP-C60-oligo-PPEs (Up spin), (h) MnP-C60-oligo-PPEs (Down spin), (i) FeP-C60-oligo-PPEs, (j) CoP-C60-oligo-PPEs (Up spin), (k) CoP-C60-oligo-PPEs (Down spin) (l) NiP-C60-oligo-PPEs, (m) CuP-C60-oligo-PPEs (Up spin), (n) CuP-C60-oligo-PPEs (Down spin), (o) ZnP-C60-oligo-PPEs and TiO2 and electrolyte I-/I3 - using by DFT/B3PW91-D3 level of theory.

The structural geometry parameters; M–N bond lengths, dihedral angles, center to center (d Ct-Ct), and edge to edge distances (d Ed-Ed) of MP-C60-oligo-PPEs compounds obtained from the DFT/B3PW91-D3 (bond length in Å and bond angle in °).

ZINDO/S calculated absorption properties parameters, λ, f (oscillator strength), ε (molar absorption coefficient), HOMO/LUMO orbitals involved in the transitions and light-harvesting efficiency (LHE) of MP-C60-oligo-PPEs compounds.

head.ce:title: Computational investigation on the geometry and electronic structures and absorption spectra of metal-porphyrin-oligo- phenyleneethynylenes-[60] fullerene triads

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we focus our attention on the influence of 2nd-row transition metals on the structural geometries, electronic structures, and absorption characteristics of porphyrin linked with the C60 fullerene with oligo-p-phenyleneethynylenes (MP-C60-oligo-PPEs) compounds. The DFT/B3PW91-D3 and CAM-B3LYP-D3/6-31G (d) calculations revealed that various metals embedded within the porphyrin moiety give different bridge conformations and different HOMO-LUMO energy levels. We calculate the UV–Vis spectra and absorption parameters using the time-dependent ZINDO/S approach. Our findings indicate that all the compounds have enhanced absorptions in the visible light range, and their molecular orbital energies adopt the condition of sensitizers. However, all of the complexes except down spin states exhibit considerably charge spatial separation. The results suggest that the ZnP-C60-oligo-PPEs triad can meet the necessary conditions of the sensitizer of dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) in comparison with other counterparts and could be an optimum triad compound for potential application in photovoltaic devices.

ce:section.ce:para: In general, due to the facile manufacturing procedures, substantial efficacy, and cost-effectiveness of materials, dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) have been found as one of the most desirable eco-environmentally friendly photovoltaic devices [1-3]. The conversion of solar energy performs an essential task as one of the most state-of-the-art strategies capable of replacing non-renewable sources of energy generation. The performances of DSSCs vigorously rely on various criteria, including broad ranges of absorption for the whole regions of near-infrared and visible, considerable coefficients of molar absorption, and the occupied/unoccupied molecular orbital energy levels, which respectively lead to effective electron injection and efficacious electron reduction in dye production [4]. The criteria mentioned above are closely tied with the structures of the excited and ground electronic states in the sensitizing dyes. Porphyrin-based structures have been widely scrutinized as photosensitizers for DSSCs owing to the moderate Q bands (550–750 nm) and potent Soret (400–500 nm) absorption characteristics and also their effective task in natural photosynthetic systems.

Porphyrin-based structures are frequently implemented in various systems of solar cells because of the electron transfer characteristics and light-harvesting efficiency (LHE) [5-7]. Considerable attention has been earmarked to the fullerene complexes, especially C60 nanocage with metalloporphyrins (MP) and porphyrins (P) chromophores as natural chromophores substantially absorbing in a range of visible. The molecules as mentioned earlier as π-conjugated acceptor and donor entities are extensively employed for mimicking the photosynthetic procedures. The magnificent redox- and photo-chemical features are known as one of the most broadly investigated categories of materials [8,9]. Fullerene-based structures have been employed as electron acceptors [10]. Their charge transfer has been detected over a distance analogous to natural photosystems, while porphyrins have been implemented as electron donors.

Furthermore, they have been applied to build molecular polyads, representing photo-induced electron transfer [11,12]. The marvelous feature of fullerenes and porphyrins is their remarkable ability in the spontaneous attraction of each other using orbitals interactions, as comprehensively surveyed in the solution and solid-state [13,14] and verified with the aid of high-level assessments such as B3LYP-D3/6-31G (d) and CAM-B3LYP/Def2-SVP level of theory [15,16].

Recently, it has been well-established that by combining fullerenes and porphyrin moiety, photoactive compounds will be resulted, with unique capability in transferring energy or electron upon photoexcitation [17]. The leading pathway, besides mimicking the processes included in photosynthesis, comprised of constructing various molecular assemblies such as ployads, including dyads, triads, and so forth, through covalent linkage of the electron donor and acceptors entities. Worth noting that linkers are responsible for limiting the relative distances and orientations be the acceptors and donors [17].

In these systems, solar energy is harvested for shuttling the charges within distances of around 3.5 nm in a few picoseconds [18] at excellent rates and significant effectiveness in approximately quantitative quantum outputs [19]. In such systems, the focal point on the structures of the molecules can mediate charges between the acceptors and donor species, which renders the possibility of controlling charge shifts/transfer for nanometer distances. It must be pointed out that the wire-like performance of these molecular structures is imperative [20]. The other essential criterion is the parameters that control the correlation of the constants of charge transfer with distance [21,22]. To achieve this goal, a great number of π-conjugated organic bridges have been developed and assessed as molecular wires, for instance, oligo (phenylenevinylene), oligo-acetylenes, oligo-phenylenes, oligo (phenylene ethynylenes) and, oligo (thiophenes) [23-27]. Furthermore, the oligomers of porphyrin have been discovered as molecular wires [28]. Efficient or inefficient wire-like performances rely on incorporating a wide spectrum of factors such as the orbitals matching, the degree of conjugation in bridges, donor-bridge energy gap, and so on [29].

Over recent years, considerable attention has been dedicated to the advancement of porphyrin and fullerene and their derivatives to ameliorate the performances of the solar cell. Guldi and colleagues explored the photo-induced charge transfer among coordination porphyrin pentamers (RuTPP·CO)4. (3-TPyP), and (RuTPP·CO)4. (4-TPyP) and C60 in the progression of photoactive porphyrin/fullerene compounds [30]. By taking advantage of density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) approaches, Cramariuc and coworkers discovered the structure, photo-absorption, and excited states of the porphyrin-fullerene dyad [31]. Sushanta et al. scrutinized the electron transfer procedure for fullerene-zinc porphyrins dyads and optical absorption investigations on complex creation [32]. Kim and coworkers evaluated the relative positions of the LUMO and HOMO levels in acceptor and donor species for porphyrin derivations with the aid of DFT and semi-empirical AM1 computations [33,34]. Moreover, Zhang and colleagues theoretically assessed a plethora of substituted porphyrins with diverse acceptor–donor combinations [35,36].

In our previous work [37], we studied the electronic structures and UV–Vis spectra of the porphyrin–ZnP duplicate system. Some transition metals were replaced instead of Zn atom using DFT and TD-ZINDO/S calculations. We found that the Ti-porphyrin triad system showed the most efficiency for dye sensitizer performance owing to its lesser chemical hardness, greater chemical potential, and electron-accepting power in comparison with other counterparts. In another theoretical study, we investigated the incorporated Li atom into the C60 fullerene of the C60–porphyrin–metalloporphyrin triad system (Li@C60–P–MP) [38]. Our findings revealed that Li@C60–P–ScP, Li@C60–P–CoP, and, Li@C60–P–MnP complexes with the negligible energy gap exhibited potential applications in photovoltaic systems with the appropriate, efficient performance of DSSC devices.

In the current research, the oligo-PPEs as molecular bridges have been thoroughly investigated owing to their incredible physicochemical characteristics and their synthetic versatility. Regarding the reports, these structures aid in the desirable conduction of charges because of their extended π-system, reliable contacts over the intermediate, substantial electron density, and long distance between electron acceptor and donor species connected to the termini of oligomers [39,40]. Particularly, when the self-assemblage of these systems occurs on the metallic surface, for instance, the gold surface, they found the ability to transfer and storing negative charges through molecular orbitals [41].

It has been demonstrated that a particular planar configuration can result in great wire-like behaviors. In contrast, the rearrangements of the phenyl rings that belong to the bridge in an orthogonal manner can attenuate the wire-like behaviors [29,42]. In practice, such conformations can perturb the conjugations of π-π between the single sub-units of the molecular bridges. Furthermore, matching the energy levels of the orbital within the single component is equally essential, which is responsible for simplifying the transfer of electrons via the bridge, empowering the strategy of charge-hopping. As an alternative, photo-induced charge separation can be controlled through a mechanism of superexchange. According to the literature, in similar exTTF-oligo-PPE-C60 structures [43,44], the influences of exchanging the metalloporphyrins instead of exTTF as electron donors were considered.

To attain deep insight into the photovoltaic features, starting with excited states, both Zn-based porphyrin and the correspondent complexes of transition metals were meticulously investigated. In the current research, by taking advantage of theoretical studies, photovoltaic features of the triad systems of MP-C60 -oligo-PPEs will be predicted, and their prospective efficacy in solar cell applications will be ascertained. We concentrate on the structural geometries employing DFT-D3 and relevant spectral and electrical characteristics with semi-empirical ZNIDO/S techniques. It is expected that the obtained findings can present novel perspectives for the practical application of solar cells.

All assessments were executed by employing the quantum chemistry code Orca ver. 3.0 [45]. Geometries optimizations and electronic structure calculations were performed using the DFT method with the B3PW91 functionals, which considers Becke’s three parameters exchange functional combined with the Parr and Wang’s correlation functional [46,47]. Furthermore, the long-range density hybrid functional, namely the CAM-B3LYP-D3 level of theory, was selected for evaluating electronic structure [48]. We used the 6-31G (d) Pople type basis set for molecular systems under investigation. We employed the atom pair-wise additive formalism implemented in Grimme methodology [49,50], the D3BJ scheme, to describe the long-range dispersion interactions. The DFT-D3 method has been shown to perform well with the Becke-type exchange [51]. We also used the [SD (X, MDF)] effective core potential (ECP) scheme for the transition metals [52,53].

Meanwhile, the quantum chemistry-based semi-empirical ZINDO/S method with reasonable computational costs and good results for large molecular systems was used for excited states and absorption spectra calculations. The ZINDO/S is based on the Hartree-Fock-based single configuration interaction, which is well-implemented in the Orca software [54-57] and is a powerful tool for understanding the electronic transitions in molecular systems well as orbital involved in the corresponding transitions [58]. It has been parameterized appropriately to provide the UV–Vis electronic transition states, while cannot optimize the structural geometries, accurately [59-61]. We used the time-dependent (TD) scheme for ZINDO/S calculations to investigate the vertical excited-states energy levels, electronic, and absorption spectra theoretically. ZINDO/S is well-parameterized for molecular systems with various types of atoms involving heavy metals [62,63,64,65,66]. It is highly noted that ZINDO/S analysis of absorption spectra agrees well with both experimental and other theoretical data for similar molecular systems [66-69]. The Gabedit [70] package was used to visualize the results obtained from the Orca code. It is a powerful tool associated with the analysis, converting, and editing of molecular systems and displaying UV–Vis, IR, Raman spectra, and electronic circular dichroism spectrum.

From the theoretical point of view, computational methods based on quantum mechanics such as DFT and semiempirical methods have been widely utilized for studying structural geometry as well as electronics structures of molecular systems. First, we optimized the bare fullerene C60 nanocage, and the calculated C-C bond lengths fussing the pentagon–hexagon and the hexagon–hexagon rings are determined to be about 1.452 Å and 1.394 Å, respectively, which consist well with the experimental data (1.458 and 1.401 Å) [71-74]. Then, the considered supramolecules are fully optimized, and the calculated geometric parameters are presented in Table 1 and Fig. 1 a.

It is well-known that the corresponding vibrational frequencies of the optimized molecular structures can appraise whether the achieved geometries are real minimum criteria [75,76]. In order to overcome the expensive calculation costs for the supramolecules under investigation, we have to consider three separate sections of the molecular system i.e., the C60 fullerene, the oligo-PPEs, and the MP (M: Zn). The optimization of selected separate parts has been carried out at the same level of theory, and then the vibrational frequencies of the molecular systems were accomplished. The B3PW91-D3/6-31G (d) calculation outcomes indicated that all the determined frequencies for three considered segments have positive values, and the imaginary frequencies did not appear. Furthermore, the estimated frequencies for all the separated molecular systems agree well with the corresponding values presented in the literature [77,78]. These findings reveal that the optimized geometries possess the correct minimum and reach a stable state thermodynamically.

Our first-principles findings for MP-C60-oligo-PPEs compounds show that the center-to-center (d Ct-Ct) distance, the distance between the metal center of the MP and the center of C60 nanocage, was found to be the largest value in the FeP-C60-oligo-PPEs system while the CuP-C60-oligo-PPEs has the smallest Ct-Ct distance. Further, the calculated edge-to-edge (d Ed-Ed) distance, the distance between the porphyrin and the C60 skeleton, for the ZnP-C60-oligo-PPEs /FeP-C60-oligo-PPEs system was found to be the largest/smallest distance amongst the MP-C60-oligo-PPEs supramolecules. The closer distance between the porphyrin and the fullerene moiety leads to the faster excited-state deactivation of the device than other counterparts [79]. The bonding distances between the metals and N atoms in the porphyrin are estimated to be about 2 Å for all compounds under study, which are comparable with the reported theoretical and experimental values for similar compounds [80-85]. The dihedral angle between the phenyl rings and the bridge entity tends to take on almost planar conformation in the NiP-C60-oligo-PPEs system (1.5°), which reveals an enriched π-conjugation all over the oligo-PPEs linker. It is noted that the dihedral angle between the porphyrins and the bridge affected the electronic communication between donor and acceptor moieties and hence the charge-recombination rates. Indeed, as the dihedral angle reduced, thus a stronger conjugation between the bridge and the porphyrin happened, which leads to an enhancement in electronic communication between donor and acceptor and hence the shorter charge-recombination rates. According to the optimized structures, the ScP-C60-oligo-PPEs compound shows the shortest charge-recombination rates owing to its coplanarity between the porphyrin and oligo-PPEs.

For comparison and evaluation of the accuracy of the cost-effective ZINDO/S method in structural optimization of supramolecules, we optimized the ZnP-C60-oligo-PPEs compound by this semi-empirical level of theory. The optimized structure is shown in Fig. 1b. It can be seen in the figure that the molecular structure was destroyed after optimization, the fullerene cage formed new bonds with the ZnP moiety, and the whole structure was crumpled. The present result confirms the other studies [59-61] regarding the drawbacks of the ZINDO/S method for structural optimization of such systems despite its low-cost computational cost.

We now study the UV–Vis absorption spectra, as a principal factor for the proper performance of sensitizers in DSSCs. As the role of the thumb, the higher molar extinction coefficient of sensitizer results in the optimum efficiency of conversion of light to energy in absorbance of visible/near-IR region.

The estimated absorption wavelength, λabs, for the considered triads was found to be 400–600 nm, as given in Table 2 , and the corresponding spectra are represented in Fig. 2. We should mention that transitions from the HOMO to the LUMO (H -> L) have been considered for signals related to the maximum wavelength, λmax, as given in Table 2. The ZINDO/S estimated values of UV–Vis spectra for triad systems under study are shown in Fig. 2. It was found that the maximum absorption peaks for ZnP-C60-oligo-PPEs, TiP-C60-oligo-PPEs, NiP-C60-oligo-PPEs, FeP-C60-oligo-PPEs, CuP-C60-oligo-PPEs, CrP-C60-oligo-PPEs, and CoP-C60-oligo-PPEs appear in the range of 410–460 nm while the VP-C60-oligo-PPEs, MnP-C60-oligo-PPEs, and ScP-C60 -oligo-PPEs compounds demonstrate a maximum absorption peak at 578.60, 569.70 and 566.50 nm respectively. According to our findings for the ZnP-C60-oligo-PPEs system, two excited states were predicted at 425.9 and 440.1 nm with the oscillator strengths (f) of 1.31 and 2.61 as well as the molar absorption coefficients (ε) of 18.35 and 37.85 respectively. The corresponding configurations of the considered excitation were found as H-3 → L + 3 (34.62%) and H → L + 1 (18.58%) in 425.9 nm and H-3 → L + 1 (25.30%) and H → L + 3 (22.81%) in 440.1 nm being responsible for the observed absorptions. A similar trend has been observed for other components under study, as presented in Table 2. Furthermore, the observed absorption spectra reveal that MnP-C60-oligo-PPEs possess the maximum molar absorption coefficient value (71.82) and hence would be more suitable for energy conversion. The light-harvesting efficiency (LHE) as the performance of triads in response to the event lights plays a key role in their efficiency. Indeed, the higher LHE value leads to the optimum photocurrent response of triad compounds according to the following expression [93]:

Where f denotes the oscillator strength and can be determined from TD-based evaluations such as the ZINDO/S model of the theory, from Eq. (1), one can see that the LHE directly relates to the oscillator strength and the molar extinction coefficient of the respective sensitizer.

The LHE parameters obtained with ZINDO/S for all MP-C60-oligo-PPEs systems under evaluation are listed in Table 2. The LHE parameter for dye compounds such as porphyrin ranged from around 0.9 to 1, while for the poor chromophores, it was to be found around 0.5 [94,95]. In the meantime, it was also found that the highest oscillator strength belongs to MnP-C60-oligo-PPEs (3.83) at a wavelength of 569.70 nm, whereas the lowermost is related to the TiP-C60-oligo-PPEs compounds with the f value of 0.22 and wavelength of 451.80 nm. The corresponding parameters for the ZnP-C60-oligo-PPEs triad are at 2.61 of oscillator strength and wavelengths of 440.10 nm. From these findings, one can see that when different metals were substituted instead of Zn in porphyrin moiety, both the maximum molar absorption coefficient and oscillator strength have been changed. As a consequence, it is presented that Mn substituted system demonstrates the highest oscillator strength and hence the optimum light-harvesting efficiency, therefore foremost to higher triad efficiency.

We next study the localization of HOMO/LUMO states across the moieties of the compounds. As it appears, the semi-empirical outcomes fail to provide reasonable predictions on the HOMO/LUMO density analyses [96] thus, we have employed a more sophisticated method at the B3PW91-D3/6-31G (d) level of theory which can give reliable results for such supramolecular systems [79]. Our first-principles analysis of the electronic structure showed that the majority of the HOMOs (highest occupied molecular orbital) is located on the porphyrin as an electron-donating moiety, and the LUMOs (lowest unoccupied molecular orbital) are localized on the C60 cage skeleton as an electron-accepting moiety for all compounds (Fig. 4 ). We can see that the electron-accepting feature of the fullerene nanocage turns out to be completely well-maintained. It should be noted that the HOMO/LUMO states obtained here for the ZnP-C60-oligo-PPEs system agrees with previous theoretical calculation with the same level of theory, B3PW91-D3/6-31G (d), which indicates the reliable results obtained with calculation methods employed in the present work. It is noted that for the complexes with unpaired electrons, the configuration of electrons involves up and down spin states, ScP-C60-oligo-PPEs (s 2 d 1), VP-C60-oligo-PPEs (s 2 d 3), MnP-C60-oligo-PPEs (s 2 d 5), CoP-C60-oligo-PPEs (s 2 d 7) and CuP-C60-oligo-PPEs (s 1 d 10) compound in this study. In these systems, for the up spin states, the HOMO and the LUMO states are localized on the porphyrin and C60 cage, accordingly. In contrast, for the down spin states, both the HOMO and the LUMO states are accommodated on the porphyrin moiety as can be seen from Fig. 4.

Moreover, the toning of the HOMO/LUMO energy levels of the compounds performs an essential task in effective electronic communication concerning the donor and acceptor moieties which can be verified through the electrochemical analysis. Our estimated HOMO/LUMO energy levels of the ZnP-C60-oligo-PPEs system are −5.170/-3.663 eV, with the HOMO-LUMO gap of 1.507 eV, which consists well with the potential experimental gap for the redox processes (1.6 eV) [79].

Two important factors affect the efficiency of DSSCs. The first is the spatial charge separation, which can decrease the electron-hole recombination rate and can be evaluated through the charge spatial separation of HOMO and LUMO. Another factor is the effective electron transfer from the MP-C60-oligo-PPEs triad to the electrode [97,98]. A well-organized sensitizer should have clear spatial charge separation. Consequently, the effective spatial charge separation can result in lower chances of recombination of charge carriers. Based on the obtained results, the HOMOs of all the complexes and up spin states are mainly situated on MP, which implicates that the MP is the electron contributor for the optical absorption transitions of all these complexes, whereas the LUMOs are focused on the C60 entity as the acceptors. Hence, the spatial charge separation in these systems is recognized. With a glance at the results, the carrier recombination is slow, which is favorable to the conversion efficiency of DSSCs. Unfortunately, the favorable outcomes were not obtained for down spin states. Based on the obtained results the, both HOMOs and LUMOs of the complexes are accumulated around the MP portion, and consequently, no obvious charge spatial separation is observed.

We now evaluate the quantum descriptor parameters by calculating the energy gap in the triad systems considered that performs a substantial task in the photocurrent efficiency of such compounds. The energy gap is described as the difference between energy levels of the uppermost of the valence state (HOMO) and the lowermost of the conduction one (LUMO). Consequently, the larger bandgap leads to the hardship of moving electrons from the HOMO state to the LUMO state, which defines the reactivity of molecular systems. Indeed, the HOMO energy is related to the lowest ionization potential energy (EI) of a system, while the energy of the LUMO usually is almost the estimated as the electron affinity (EA) of molecular systems.

Like previous research, forecasting the difference between the EI and the EA, directly from the difference between the HOMO and the LUMO of a DFT method, is having trouble, as pure Kohn − Sham approximations cannot estimate the correct fundamental gap [99,100]. The generalized Kohn − Sham scheme offers the framework to address the fundamental gap challenge in DFT technics through which range-separated hybrid (RSH) methods such as CAM-B3LYP-D3/6-31G (d) have been progressed [101,102]. The bandgaps of triad compounds in the present study have been estimated by employing the B3PW91-D3/6-31G (d) level of theory and compared with the results obtained from CAM-B3LYP-D3/6-31G (d) method as shown in Supplementary Information. (Fig. S1-S2 and Table. S1).

We found that in these complexes, with the presence of transition metal in the compounds, the B3PW91-D3/6-31G (d) level of theory provides good results as accurate as the experimental data HOMO-LUMO bandgap [79] compared to CAM-B3LYP-D3/6-31G (d) functional as an RSH procedure.

The obtained HOMO-LUMO bandgaps of the compounds under consideration showed that the CuP-C60-oligo-PPEs and the ScP-C60-oligo-PPEs systems respectively have the largest and smallest gaps compared with the other counterparts. The orders in the energy gaps trend follow the hierarchy:

CuP-C60-oligo-PPEs > CoP-C60-oligo-PPEs > FeP-C60-oligo-PPEs > NiP-C60-oligo-PPEs > MnP-C60-oligo-PPEs > VP-C60-oligo-PPEs > ZnP-C60-oligo-PPEs > CrP-C60-oligo-PPEs > TiP-C60-oligo-PPEs > ScP-C60-oligo-PPEs (2).

The HOMO and LUMO levels of a sensitizer of DSSCs must satisfy the electrode and electrolyte necessities, and simultaneously, the sensitizer is involved in electronic transmission, in which the light absorption consequences in transferring the excited electrons of MP-C60-oligo-PPEs triad to the surface of the semiconductor electrode. In the present research, the electrode of TiO2 and the electrolyte of pair I−/I3 −are employed as a paradigm to assess the feasibility of the MP-C60-oligo-PPEs triad as a sensitizer.

It is worth mentioning that ZnP-C60-oligo-PPEs, NiP-C60-oligo-PPEs, CoP-C60-oligo-PPEs, FeP-C60-oligo-PPEs, MnP-C60-oligo-PPEs, CuP-C60-oligo-PPEs, ScP-C60-oligo-PPEs (Down spin), and VP-C60-oligo-PPEs (Up spin) compounds seem to be appropriate for photosensitizer applications owing to their lower HOMO level rather than the redox potential one (about −4.8 eV for I−/I3 −electrolyte) to realize the sensitizer regeneration [103] efficiently. Unfortunately, the HOMO energy levels of ScP-C60-oligo-PPEs (Up spin), VP-C60-oligo-PPEs (Down spin), TiP-C60-oligo-PPEs, and CrP-C60-oligo-PPEs are above the redox potential of I−/I3 − electrolyte, which means that they do not meet the requirements of DSSCs. Furthermore, the estimated LUMO levels of all compounds were found to be rarely larger than −4.0 eV, which belongs to the conduction band (CB) level of TiO2 [104]. This factor warrants the facility of electron injection from the dye to the TiO2 moiety accompanied by the triad regeneration by gaining electrons from the electrolyte, as depicted in Fig. 5 . The variation of bandgaps for various metal substituted ZnP-C60-oligo-PPEs compounds are listed in Table 3 and Fig. 4.

We found that among all complexes which can meet the requirement of the electrode of TiO2 and the electrolyte of pair I−/I3 −a as a pattern as a sensitizer the ZnP-C60-oligo-PPEs possess smaller HOMO-LUMO bandgaps. It should be mentioned that the larger bandgap introduces a disadvantage in its application for solar cells. It is recognized that the narrower bandgap of a compound results in a greater short-circuit current density (JSC) through enhancement of the harvesting of sunlight [105]. The JSC factor is defined as the maximum achieved currents in solar cells, which relates to the amount of harvested photon light. Consequently, the high value of JSC is desired for solar cell devices. Based on the majority of charge injections for the efficient performance of devices under investigation, the ZnP-C60-oligo-PPEs system owing to meet the demands, is forecasted to be the best compound for DSSC applications.

Quantum mechanics, as a powerful tool, can provide valuable data about the reactivity of chemical compounds. Molecular properties such as softness/hardness, electronegativity, and, electrophilicity provide useful information about the reactivity of molecular systems. Among these, chemical hardness provides more useful information about the chemical properties of molecular systems and demonstrates the resistance of intramolecular charge transfer of compounds [106,107]. Softness, on the other hand, is defined as the mutual of hardness [108]. Chemical hardness, η, can be determined by the following equation:
(3)
η ≈
( I P - E A )
2
≈
( E L U M O - E H O M O )
2

From Eq. (3), it was found that when the chemical hardness was increased, then, the conversion efficiency decreased. As a result, the optimum compound for superior charge transfer and desired JSC value was achieved by the lower values of hardness. From Table 3 one can see that the CuP-C60-oligo-PPEs (Up Spin) have the maximum chemical hardness value (0.996), which causes the lowest resistance against the charge transfer in the intramolecular system. Also, the order of chemical hardness for considered compounds is as follows:

CuP-C60-oligo-PPEs > CoP-C60-oligo-PPEs > FeP-C60-oligo-PPEs > NiP-C60-oligo-PPEs > MnP-C60-oligo-PPEs > VP-C60-oligo-PPEs > ZnP-C60-oligo-PPEs > CrP-C60-oligo-PPEs > TiP-C60-oligo-PPEs > ScP-C60-oligo-PPEs (4).

The chemical hardness trend obtained shows that the ZnP-C60-oligo-PPEs compound has a lower hardness than other triad counterparts, which can be eligible in DSSCs and hence the most light-harvesting property as an improved chromophore.

The ability of molecular systems to electron-accepting, known as electrophilicity (ω), as defined by Parr et al. [109], is a global reactivity factor that indicates the stabilization energy of the compound. The power in electron acceptance demonstrates superior ability in charge accepting [110]. Compounds with high electron-accepting characteristics are desired, which accept electrons from the donor moiety, namely, the greater values for ω and ω+ are highly desirable for solar cell applications. In this study, the electrophilicity is estimated using the below expression:
(5)
ω =
μ
2
2 η
(6)
ω + =
( I + 3 A )
2
16 ( I - A )
Where μ, η, I, and A denote chemical potential, chemical hardness, ionization potential energy, and electron affinity, respectively.
The electron-accepting power, ω+, for compounds under study is calculated with the B3PW91-D3/6-31G (d), and the obtained indexes are given in Table 3.

Among the compounds that can be suitable in DSSCs, ZnP-C60-oligo-PPEs possess the uppermost power for electron tendency from the acceptor moiety of the triad compound with the largest value of ω+ (10.829) eV). It is noted that a high value of ω+ leads to the desired JSC index for the respective compound. The predicted ω+ values for triads systems under study are as follows:

ScP-C60-oligo-PPEs > VP-C60-oligo-PPEs > TiP-C60-oligo-PPEs > CrP-C60-oligo-PPEs > ZnP-C60-oligo-PPEs > MnP-C60-oligo-PPEs > NiP-C60-oligo-PPEs > CoP-C60-oligo-PPEs > FeP-C60-oligo-PPEs > CuP-C60-oligo-PPEs (7).

Meanwhile, electrophilicity exhibits a similar tendency manner as ω+, therefore, ZnP-C60-oligo-PPEs appear to be energetically stable by gaining electrons from the surroundings. From the reported results elsewhere, one can find that the higher chemical softness and electron-accepting power leads to an enhanced JSC index, and hence the desired efficiency for light conversion [111]. Further, the chemical potential (μ) as other molecular properties discloses the receiving propensity of electrons from a system in an equilibrium state. For lower chemical potential values, thus the molecular systems are more stable or less reactive. From the determined chemical potential as presented in Table 3, the ZnP-C60-oligo-PPEs compound (-4.417 eV) has the maximum value among the other counterparts. A higher μ value and low η value define the strong electrophilic characteristics of the compound.

We can conclude that among the complexes that can be qualified in DSSCs, ZnP-C60-oligo-PPEs is the most efficient dye sensitizer for DSSCs due to the ZnP-C60-oligo-PPEs complex has a lower chemical hardness, gap energy as well as higher chemical potential and electron-accepting power among other complexes. Narrowing the HOMO–LUMO gap of a complex can harvest more sunlight, which leads to a higher short-circuit current density (JSC) [105] which represents the maximum current that could be achieved in a solar cell which depends on the number of absorbed photons. We can infer that the high values of the JSC are desirable. Furthermore, the lower values of hardness are suggested as the best compound for charge transfer and hence a better short-circuit current density (JSC). The previous research revealed that the lower chemical hardness and higher electron-accepting power, the better JSC, and accordingly a better light conversion efficiency [111].

We have used computational tools to investigate the structural, electronic, and photovoltaic characteristics of a series of novel MP-C60-oligo-PPEs triad compounds with M consisting of the second-row transition metals to evaluate the potential applications of compounds considered in solar cells. The first-principles calculations at the DFT/B3PW91-D3/6-31G (d) and CAM-B3LYP-D3/6-31G (d) level of theory are used for geometries optimization and electronic structure analysis, at the same time the absorption spectra have been estimated by employing the semiempirical ZINDO/S calculation.

The optimized geometries show a non-planar conformation of bridge-phenyl and phenyl-porphyrin for all systems, which affects the extended π-conjugation feature of compounds under study. In addition, the electronic structure analysis indicated a robust location of the HOMOs on the porphyrin as an electron-donating center and the LUMOs on the C60 nanocage as electron-accepting moiety (except for the down spin states). Indeed, the electron-accepting feature of the C60 fullerene is turned out to be well-kept, except for the down spin states of complexes. Further, the matching of the HOMO-LUMO energy gap for the ZnP-C60-oligo-PPEs compound as the reference system is proved by the electrochemical data. The excited-state levels and UV–vis absorption accompanied by spectral parameters like λ max, f, and, LHE have been estimated. The studied spectra found between 400 and 700 nm originated from transitions with the optimum LHE values for all compounds, close to the one. The HOMO and LUMO energy levels of ZnP-C60-oligo-PPEs satisfy the necessary conditions of the typical DSSCs with TiO2 and I−/I3 − as the electrode and the electrolyte, respectively, and could be an optimum triad compound for potential application in photovoltaic devices.

On the other hand, the quantum molecular descriptors such as chemical softness/hardness and electrophilicity give a deeper insight into anticipating the triad compounds with the optimum effectiveness. Indeed, the higher softness and electrophilicity, which leads to the higher electron-accepting power, are desirable. Our DFT/B3PW91-D3 based findings revealed that the change of transition metals had a significant effect on the properties of the triad compounds. In the case of the ZnP-C60-oligo-PPEs compound, the system exhibited the highest electrophilicity and chemical softness. The present effort suggests that metal substituting in MP moiety of triad systems is a suitable way to tune the efficiency of MP-C60-oligo-PPEs compounds and provides useful data for further theoretical and experimental investigation on the development of such compounds.
